title: Investigating the effect of the 5A self-management care model on the caregiver's burden in the aged with peptic ulcers: Randomized controlled Trial (RCT).
abstract: Background As individuals age, the functionality of internal organs declines. Gastrointestinal disorders are prevalent in elderlies and significantly impact their caregivers. Hence, this research examined the impact of the 5A self-management care model on the caregiver's care burden in aged with peptic ulcers. Materials and methods In 2024 in Ilam (Iran), a double-blind randomized controlled trial was done on seventy informal elderly caregivers who were randomly divided into control and intervention groups with permuted blocks. The participants in the intervention group received eight weeks of the 5A care model, but the control group just implemented routine care. The Zarit Burden Index was completed before and two weeks after the end of the intervention. Statistical tests included the Chi-square, paired t , and, independent t in the level of significance considered less than 0.05 and analyzed in SPSS V.16. Results The mean and standard deviation of the age of the caregivers who finished the study were 42.11 ± 5.37. The mean and standard deviation score of the caregiver's care burden improved in the intervention group but decayed in the control group ( P = 0.011). Before the intervention, no significant difference was observed between both groups in the caregiver's scores; However, there was a significant difference two weeks after the end of the intervention ( P = 0.028). Conclusion Implementing the 5A self-management care model reduces the burden of care for the caregivers of elderly people with peptic ulcers. So, this care model minimizes the pressure on the caregiver in social, personal, emotional, and economic dimensions.

The mean and standard deviation score of the caregiver's care burden improved in the intervention group but decayed in the control group ( P = 0.011). journal: Journal of education and health promotion
citation: Journal of education and health promotion. 2025;14:521

full_text_sections.INTRODUCTION: Introduction Due to the progress and development of health issues, reaching old age is an integral part of people’s lives, It is caused by human growth and movement around the axis of time, which is considered one of the most important issues in the current century.[ 1 ] In 2019, their population was reported to be 1 billion people, which is expected to reach more than 1.2 billion people by 2050. In Iran, it is predicted that the elderly population will be more than 25 million people by 2030.[ 2 ] Aging in itself is not a disease, but because aging causes internal organs to undergo functional changes, more than 80% of the elderly suffer from at least one chronic disease that affects various aspects of their lives.[ 2 ] Heart disease, types of cancer, diabetes, chronic respiratory diseases, muscular and skeletal diseases, digestive disorders, and kidney diseases are the most common chronic diseases in the elderly.[ 3 ] Aging can affect the efficiency of all body organs, including the digestive system.[ 4 ] No gastrointestinal disease is specific to old age, but the prevalence of some of them increases with age, and they need more management[ 5 ] In the elderly, a series of major changes occur in the stomach due to decreased blood flow in the stomach area and decreased defense and repair mechanisms.[ 5 ] Among the digestive disorders in the elderly, the most common disorder is peptic ulcer, which causes corrosion and damage to the peptic mucosa and is three times more common in the elderly than in other populations.[ 6 ] According to the results of many studies, this disorder is directly related to gender, aging, and smoking, and it has caused a lot of money to be spent on its treatment every year.[ 7 ] At least 10% of the population suffering from peptic ulcers is over 65 years old, and this number continues to rise annually, making this disorder one of the leading causes of death in the elderly.[ 8 ] H elicobacter P ylori infections, non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) like aspirin, and serotonin reuptake inhibitor drugs are some of the most common reasons why older people get peptic ulcers. Physiological changes that come with aging, such as decreased bicarbonate, mucin, and prostaglandin, can also worsen the disease. For this reason, obtaining a detailed history of non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug use and a previous history of any Helicobacter pylori infection is essential.[ 9 ] Upper endoscopy can be used to diagnose this disease, especially in people who are over 65 years old and have a positive family history of weight loss, early satiety, and iron deficiency anemia.[ 10 ] Treatment methods for peptic ulcer healing include drug therapy, surgery, and control of intervention factors such as nutrition, exercise, and stress reduction.[ 11 ] Therefore, these patients must adhere to their treatment regimen, follow their medication plan, maintain a healthy diet, and control their stress levels. Therefore, to prevent the occurrence of medical and surgical complications in the elderly, it is necessary that their caregivers (formal and informal caregivers), in general, the people who have the most interaction in caring for the elderly, have maximum compatibility with They create disease limitations for the elderly and impose the burden of care on their caregivers.[ 12 ] Caregivers can be divided into two categories: formal and trained caregivers, who receive a salary for their work, and informal caregivers, such as family members who have not received special training.[ 13 ] Caring burden means the pressure that is experienced during caring for a person with a chronic disease or an old family member such as the elderly, and it increases the care burden for the elderly with a chronic disease and their families.[ 13 ] The care burden of most people with chronic and dependent diseases is borne by a family member.[ 2 ] Among the reasons why informal caregivers are used more are the high cost of care in private centers, the lack of government rehabilitation centers, and the lack of follow-up after discharge, which makes families volunteer for care.[ 14 ] Due to the high burden of caring for these patients, the lives of caregivers are subject to changes such as lack of time, loss of emotion, feeling of losing control over personal life,[ 15 ] increased burden of care, and suffering from various acute and chronic diseases.[ 16 ] Currently, as technology expands, various non-pharmacological, self-management, and educational treatments are available at very low costs.[ 17 ] These treatments aim to reduce treatment costs, ease the care burden on caregivers, encourage active participation from people with chronic diseases, prevent symptom exacerbation, and promote a pleasant lifestyle.[ 18 19 ] The lifestyle and attainment of a pleasant life have become increasingly important.[ 19 ] Self-management encompasses a collection of information, an interest in self-care,[ 20 ] and an individual’s capacity to comprehend and identify these activities.[ 21 22 ] Nurses hold a unique position in communicating with patients and their caregivers, serving as consultants and guides, one of their roles is to familiarize patients with various self-management methods for chronic disease.[ 14 ] One of the common types of self-management models is the 5A model which empowerment model is an evidence-based program that covers a wide range of behavior changes in various health conditions in primary care, from chronic diseases to final treatment.[ 23 ] This method, one of the practical models in the nursing field, includes five steps: assess, advise, agree, assist, and arrange. The first stage examines the beliefs, convictions, and descriptions of the patient’s condition. The second stage focuses on guiding patients toward health and reducing personal health risks from unhealthy behaviors. In the third stage, agreement and compromise are made with patients concerning the actual selection of goals and prediction of problems. In the fourth stage, some patients need counseling and guidance regarding developing action plans, which is done through dedicated assistance. In the fifth or last stage, follow-up and support are done with different methods, such as phone calls or consecutive meetings.[ 24 ] The main goal of this program is for the elderly to reach maximum independence in decision-making, relying on their abilities and reducing the burden on caregivers.[ 25 ] Based on the results of the studies, the self-management method based on the 5A’s can reduce the intensity of fatigue and shortness of breath in people with chronic respiratory problems, increase the ability of the elderly with diabetes, and improve the quality of life in people with a history of acute syndrome coronary.[ 25 26 ] According to the mentioned above, no study has been conducted in the field of aged populations, their caregivers’ responsibility, related dimensions, and, gastrointestinal disorders based on the 5A care model to investigate the effect of the self-management program based on the 5A self-management care model on the care burden of the caregivers of the aged with gastric ulcers. Therefore, the present study was designed and implemented to assess this issue.
full_text_sections.MATERIALS AND METHODS: Materials and Methods Study design and setting This was a double-blinded randomized clinical trial which conducted in 2024. The study population was elderly referring to Gastric Clinic of Razi Hospital in Ilam province, using convenience sampling method by considering inclusive criteria such the age of 65 years and above for the elderly person and the age of the caregiver above 18 years, the ability to read and write in the elderly person and his caregiver, the presence of at least one ulcer in the Stomach by endoscopy, the presence of Helicobacter pylori infection in the biopsy sample, have first or second family relation of caregiver to elderly person at home, obtaining a score of 24 and above in Mini-Mental Status Examination (MMSE), obtaining a score of 91 and above in Barthel Index (BI), as well as exclusion criteria including unwillingness to participate in the research, more than one session of absence in training classes, a positive H. pylori antigen test at the end of the intervention, and hospitalization or death of an elderly person or his/her caregiver during the study. All of the mentioned criteria and the participant’s diagnosis and assessment for peptic ulcer were done by a gastroenterologist. Study participants and sampling According to the research by Khoshkhoo et al .[ 23 ] which the mean and standard deviation of caregivers’ care burden scores before intervention were 50.95 ± 4.77 and after intervention were 46.67 ± 5.83 based on the comparison of two mean formula by considering the first error type of 0.05, the power of 0.90 that reported 32 participants for each group. So, by accounting an attrition rate of 10%, the eventual number of participants in each group was determined 35. N = ( Z 1-α∕2 + Z 1-β ) 2 (σ 1 2 + σ 2 2 )/( m 1 - m 2 ) 2 N = (1.96 + 1.282) 2 ((4.77) 2 + (5.83) 2 )/(50.95 – 46.67) 2 = 32* %10 = 35 After meeting the inclusion or exclusion criteria, participants entered to study and were randomly divided into control (C) and intervention groups (I) by five permuted blocks randomization. When 1 st participant entered the study, he/she chose one of the sealed envelopes and determined himself/herself. Four participants reported the observed sequence to the clinic center secretary, who didn’t know the intervention’s goals. The secretary took the envelope after noting that sequence. Next, the 6 th participant chose another envelope for sequence determination, repeating this until the final participant allocation. Participants and researchers are guaranteed no allocation. The expert in training and supervising participants assessed any ambiguity or disturbance in the intervention and did not know the group allocation. The statistical analyst received the coded data after the research without knowing the kind of intervention. The primary outcome was caregiver care burden which was assessed by the Zarit Burden Index (ZBI). Data collection tools and technique 1. Demographic From This tool included the variables of age, sexuality, marital status, relation, level of education, occupation, and, daily care hours. 2. Mini-Mental Status Examination (MMSE) This tool was designed by Folste in et al . to evaluate cognitive impairment in the elderly and it has 11 parts and 19 questions. Parts number 1,2 have ten questions, and each correct answer obtains one score. Part number 4 has included one question that scores from zero to five; Parts numbers 3, 5, and 8 have one question that scores from zero to three; Question number 6 scores from zero to two; and questions numbers 7, 9, 10, and 11 just score zero to one. All questions have total scores ranging from 1 to 30. A total score of 24 and above indicates the absence of cognitive impairment, a score of 18 to 23 indicates the presence of MCI, a score of 10 to 17 indicates moderate cognitive impairment and a score of less than 10 indicates AD. To check the validity and reliability of the original version of this tool with a cut-off point of 24, the Content Validity Ratio (CVR) was 0.87 and the Content Validity Index (CVI) was 0.89.[ 27 ] In examining the validity and reliability of this tool in Persian, CVR was 0.84 and CVI was 0.92.[ 28 ] 3. Zarit Burden Index (ZBI) This tool was designed by Zarit et al .[ 29 ] and included 22 questions scored on a Likert scale from never (zero) to always (four) to measure the caring pressure of caregivers. This tool has four dimensions which include social, personal, emotional, and economic. The minimum and maximum scores for each person are between 0 and 88, and a higher score indicates more caregiver pressure, whereas lower scores indicate less pressure on caregivers. The division of scores in this questionnaire is as follows: scores from 0 to 20 indicate no caregiving pressure, scores from 21 to 40 indicate mild caregiving pressure, scores from 41 to 60 indicate moderate caregiving pressure, and scores from 61 to 88 indicate severe caregiving pressure in the individual. To check the validity and reliability of the original version of this tool, CVR was 0.77 and CVI was 0.79. In examining the validity and reliability of this tool in Persian, CVR was 0.83 and CVI was 0.89. 4. Barthel Index (BI) This tool which was designed by Barthel et al .[ 30 ] to examine the level of independence in people and has ten components that include eating, bathing, personal hygiene, dressing, stool control, urine control, use of the toilet, ambulation, the ability to walk or wheelchair, and climbing the stairs. For each of the components, a score of zero (completely dependent), one (relatively dependent), and two (completely independent) is considered. For ease of interpretation, we report the final score from 0 to 20, multiplying the final number by 5. The scoring system for this tool is as follows: a score of 100 is considered complete independence, a score of 91–99 is partial dependence, a score of 61–90 is moderate dependence, a score of 60–21 is considered severe dependence, and a score of 20–0 is considered complete dependence. To check the validity and reliability of the original version of this tool, CVR was 0.73 and CVI was 0.77. In examining the validity and reliability of this tool in Persian, CVR was 0.87 and CVI was 0.84. Intervention After allocating the participants to the control and intervention groups, the study began with a duration of eight weeks. Firstly, the demographic form and ZBI were completed by the caregivers by sending a link to the Porsline platform and then imported from Microsoft Excel 2010 to SPSS V.16 without any manipulation or access of the research team. Then, the participants of the intervention group were examined first phase (Assess), from the points of view of signs and symptoms of peptic ulcer, use of drugs, factors aggravating the condition of the disease, nutritional status, and daily activities. In the second phase (Advise), some issues were noted, which included abnormal cases and health-threatening risks related to peptic ulcers, such as an unhealthy diet, inappropriate eating time, low or insufficient physical activity level, a lack of stress management strategies, and the benefits of behavior change in preventing recurrence and, exacerbation of problems. In the third phase (Agree), a written agreement was made between the patient and the relevant researcher regarding the patient’s performance according to the individual’s specific conditions, and an operational plan was designed for each of the problems diagnosed in the previous stage. After performing endoscopy and pathology reports, assess, advise, and agree phases were performed for one hour for each patient in one day, in the presence of the patient’s caregiver. In the fourth phase (Assist), a nurse conducted a two-hour online training session for the elderly person and their caregiver. This training session was held by the presence of elderly caregivers in the online web-based service and consisted of educational slides, video clips, group discussion, and consultants, which covered topics like adhering to a diet rich in fiber and vitamins, avoiding stimulant foods, taking medications on time, abstaining from tobacco and alcohol, taking daily walks, avoiding excitement, anxiety, and stressors, preventing overeating and long-term hunger, and avoiding the use of non-prescription drugs and drug interactions. At the end of the meeting, the questions of the elderly and their caregivers were answered, and printed pamphlets were sent to them. In addition, on even days of the week until the end of the fourth week, educational materials were sent to the elderly and their caregivers in the intervention group. In the final phase (Arrange), the patients’ performance was monitored for ten weeks from the beginning of the intervention. In the first two weeks, the follow-up was done daily by calling the nurse; in the second two weeks, twice a week; and from the fourth week to the tenth week, once a week, to remind them of the continuation of the practical program. Also, the patient’s progress was monitored every four weeks during the in-person meeting in the gastroenterology clinic. In this meeting, the practical plans and goals agreed upon with the elderly with gastric ulcers were examined. The elderly in the control group just received routine measures such as periodical visits, blood pressure monitoring, blood sugar, and laboratory tests after completing the treatment processes. In the 8 th week of each participant’s presence in the study, the drugs were finished and patients went to the clinic for a prescription of laboratory H. Pylori Antigen test in stool for two weeks after the end of the PU curative course. In these two weeks, all of the participants were under supervision for correct stool exam results and guided to avoid any do any harmful interventions. After doing the test and its result, the demographic form and ZBI were completed by the caregivers by sending a link to the Porsline platform and then imported from Microsoft Excel 2010 to SPSS V.16 without any manipulation or access to the research team. Then statistical analyst received the coded data at the end of the research and without knowing the type of intervention performed, did the statistical analysis to assess the outcomes [Figures 1 and 2 ]. Figure 1 The sequence of choosing and handling the study protocol based on control and intervention groups Figure 2 The theoretical framework of 5A model in the current research as a conceptual approach Data analysis Descriptive statistics were used for the demographic data, and the variables were reported as mean, standard deviation, frequency, and percentage. Analytical statistics tests included Kolmogorov–Smironov (K-S) (for assessing the normal distribution), independent t, and Chi-square (for comparing the two groups regarding the demographic variables) and, paired t (for comparing before and after scores). All analyses were done by SPSS V.16 at a significance level of less than 0.05. Ethical considerations The ethical considerations in this study involved obtaining the ethics code from Ilam University of Medical Sciences (IR.MEDILAM.REC.1402.228), registering in the Iranian Registry of Clinical Trials (IRCT20211110053030N3), obtaining written informed consent from the research participants, ensuring confidentiality of participants’ information, not recording participants’ names on the questionnaires, assigning codes to participants, allowing participants the choice to participate or withdraw from the study, and adhering to ethical principles in collecting library, research, and human information as outlined in the Declaration of Helsinki.
full_text_sections.RESULTS: Results Sample characteristics The Kolmogorov–Smirnov (K-S) test was used to evaluate the normality of each variable in both the intervention and control groups. The test findings verified that all variables had a normal distribution. The mean and standard deviation of the total age of the caregivers was 42.11 ± 5.37. Most of the caregivers were female, married, children of the elderly, had diplomas, governmental occupation, and had daily elderly care from 4 to 8 hours. Using the Chi-square test, there wasn’t any meaningful variation between sexuality ( P = 0.086), marital status ( P = 0.577), relation ( P = 0.733), level of education ( P = 0.097), occupation ( P = 0.322) and, daily care hour ( P = 0.069) in both groups [ Table 1 ]. Table 1 Showing the frequency of caregivers by demographic variables in the intervention and control groups Variable Categories Intervention Control P # Sexuality Female 28 (%80) 24 (%) 0.086 Male 7 (%20) 11 (%) Marital Status Single 14 (%40) 10 (%) 0.577 Married 19 (%54) 25 (%) Divorced 2 (%6) 0 (%) Relation Spouse 9 (%26) 19 (%54) 0.733 Sibling 5 (%14) 3 (%9) Child 18 (%51) 9 (%26) Grandchild 3 (%9) 5 (%14) Level of Education Elementary 3 (%9) 2 (%6) 0.097 Secondary School 2 (%6) 5 (%14) High School 8 (%23) 4 (%12) Diploma 17 (%48) 15 (%42) Above Diploma 5 (%14) 9 (%26) Occupation Free 14 (%40) 11 (%32) 0.322 Governmental 18 (%51) 20 (%52) Retired 3 (%9) 4 (%12) Daily Care Hour Less than 4 hours 5 (%14) 4 (%12) 0.069 4 to 8 hours 23 (%66) 21 (%60) More than 8 hours 7 (%20) 10 (%28) # =Chi-Square test. Level of Significance was considered less than 0.05 Effectiveness of intervention There was no significant change in the mean caregiver’s care burden scores between the control and intervention groups before the intervention ( P = 0.276). However, after the intervention, this difference became significant ( P = 0.028). The independent t -test findings indicate a statistically significant disparity in the average caregiver’s care burden scores between the two groups ( P = 0.011) [ Table 2 ]. The paired t -test findings indicate that there is no statistically significant disparity in the mean score of caregiver’s care burden before and after the intervention in the control group ( P = 0.197). However, there is a notable and meaningful difference in the intervention group ( P = 0.033), as seen in Table 2 . Table 2 Comparison of the mean Caregiver’s Care Burden scores among study participants before and after the intervention in both the control and intervention groups Variable Group Independent t -test Control M±SD Intervention M±SD Caregiver’s Care Burden Before 49.18±7.63 51.07±6.39 P =0.276 After 62.91±8.67 34.59±4.86 P =0.028 Mean Difference -13.73 16.48 P =0.011 Paired t P =0.197 P =0.033 - The mean and standard deviation of the caregivers’ care burden scores show that two weeks after the end of the intervention, a significant decrease in social, personal, emotional, and economic dimensions is observed in the intervention group compared to the control group ( P < 0.001). The intergroup comparison scores show that the intervention has created a statistically significant difference in the intervention group compared to the control group ( P < 0.001). Also, the intragroup comparison shows that the intervention has caused a significant difference in all dimensions of caregivers’ care burden in the intervention group compared to the control group ( P < 0.001) [ Table 3 ]. Table 3 Comparison of the mean and standard deviation of caregivers’ care burden dimensions before and after the intervention in the control and intervention groups Variable Group M±SD Intergroup Intragroup Before After P * P # Social Pressure Control 23.64±2.79 28.84±3.09 0.703 < 0.001 Intervention 25.13±1.74 19.34±1.49 0.001 Personal Pressure Control 7.19±2.02 10.54±2.42 0.108 < 0.001 Intervention 7.55±1.05 5.69±0.86 0.001 Emotional Pressure Control 12.89±1.84 16.36±1.97 0.398 < 0.001 Intervention 14.02±2.26 6.54±1.59 0.001 Economical Pressure Control 5.46±0.98 7.17±1.19 0.514 < 0.001 Intervention 4.37±1.34 3.02±0.92 0.001 *: Paired t . # : Independent t . Level of significance was considered less than 0.05 After adjusting the effect of the pre-test, the impact of the intervention was significant ( P < 0.001) and the effect size is 0.87. In addition, the value of R 2 = 0.88 was calculated, which indicates the effectiveness of the intervention [ Table 4 ]. Table 4 Investigating the intervention effect by adjusting the pre-test effect Research step Squared mean F Effect size P Pre-test 12.14 0.18 0.002 0.644 Group 2059.84 189.31 0.87 0.001
full_text_sections.DISCUSSION: Discussion The findings of the present study showed that the mean and standard deviation of the total care burden score of caregivers and its dimensions, which include personal, social, emotional, and economic, decreased after the intervention, and this decrease was statistically significant. More clearly, the implementation of the 5A self-management care model has reduced caregivers’ care burden for the elderly with peptic ulcers. The study’s findings aligned with those of Khoshkhoo et al .,[ 23 ] which aimed to assess the impact of an intervention based on the 5A model on self-care and quality of life in older individuals with high blood pressure using a randomized clinical trial design. The data analysis indicated a considerable improvement in the elderly’s self-care pattern after the intervention. Both of these studies used the 5A self-management strategy to enhance targeted variables. Also, the research findings align with Zhang et al .’s[ 31 ] study, which examined the effects of nursing intervention 5A on the quality of life and self-care efficiency of patients undergoing chemotherapy following liver cancer surgery. The analysis of their data indicated that the intervention enhanced dimensions of quality of life, namely in the personal and emotional aspects, as well as improving self-care efficiency. Both studies are united by the significant influence of the 5A model on the variables under investigation. Besides, Sadeghigolafshanl et al .’s[ 32 ] study, which aimed to examine the impact of a self-management program using the 5A model on various aspects of quality of life in older individuals with diabetes, aligns its findings with existing research. The study findings indicate that there were significant variations in the scores for several categories of quality of life, such as personal, social, and emotional, after the intervention. The 5A model’s favorable impact on the psychosocial aspects of individuals afflicted with age-related illnesses is a shared characteristic of both studies. In addition, Moosavimoghadam et al .[ 33 ] carried out a study to investigate the impact of the 5A self-management program on medication adherence in epilepsy patients during the COVID-19 pandemic, aligning with recent research. The data analysis revealed a substantial improvement in medication adherence among patients in the intervention group after the 5A approach was deployed. Both studies are connected by the notable impact of the 5A model on the variables being studied. Moreover, the findings of this research align with the findings of the study done by Keivan et al .,[ 24 ] which was a clinical trial aimed at examining the impact of self-care using the 5A model on the quality of life of dialysis patients. These studies found that implementing the 5A model resulted in a significant improvement in the personal and social components of self-care among individuals after the intervention. However, the economic dimension of their quality of life showed no significant alteration. Both studies share the same characteristic of using the 5A care model, which has a significant impact at the end of the intervention. However, Castello et al .’s[ 34 ] study, a clinical trial that examined the impact of the 5A model on smoking cessation among hospitalized patients, did not yield the same results as current research. According to their research, the implementation of the 5A model after intervention did not result in any significant changes in the participants’ emotional and social aspects. Additionally, in the personal dimension, it led to a decrease in scores among the intervention group. Several factors contribute to the disparity, including the length of the intervention, the size of the sample, the substance of the intervention, the background circumstances of the samples, and the kind of instruments used to assess the target variables. Limitations and recommendations An inherent constraint of the research was the limited sample size, hence diminishing the extent to which the findings may be applied to the broader population. The next limitation was the rural and urban caregivers who were seen during the intervention. The study’s main strengths were the use of online mobile sessions, which declined face-to-face contact for study follow-up, and transport hazards for participants. It is suggested that future studies on larger populations and longer durations, elderly with mild to severe cognitive impairment, and chronic elderly diseases (such as autoimmune diseases, various cancers, chronic renal failure, and complications of metabolic diseases) measure the care burden of formal aged caregivers. Furthermore, by conducting training courses for geriatric specialists, geriatric nurses, and others who provide services to this age group, significant benefits can be reaped from this care model.
full_text_sections.CONCLUSION: Conclusion Implementing the 5A self-management care model reduces the burden of care for the caregivers of elderly people with peptic ulcers. As a novel method, this care model minimizes the pressure on the caregiver in social, personal, emotional, and economic dimensions. Hence, it ought to consider the fact that performing accessible, tranquilized, cost-effective, user-friendly, and easily tolerated approaches in acute or chronic illnesses affects the vast dimensions related to elderlies and health care providers. Because of elderly demands from their relatives and high treatment costs, both of them preferred to allocate home-based supervision which was accepted openly between them. By the way, for the next decades when the elderly population grows extensively, evolving curative and supportive policy managements like importing these findings from hospitals to homes can have the best achievement in health benchmarks. Not only considering these interventions as supplementary structures, policymakers can administrate sequentially for other medical staff in formal health and hygiene systems to valuable changes in caregivers’ and elderlies’ attitudes and promote services’ competency in providing care model, but also can effectively support the medical needs of elderlies and decline in non-obligated caregivers’ services. FT, MM, MS and AV: Final approval of the version to be published. FT, MM, MS and AV: Agreement to be accountable for all aspects of the work. Conflicts of interest There are no conflicts of interest.
